coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots efficiently enhance photothermal conversion and storage of organic phase change materials through interfacial interaction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinlong
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Polymer phase change materials (PCMs) often suffer from the attenuation of thermal storage capacities and the shrinkage of storage duration after adding conventional high heat conduction matters, resulting in low efficiency of heat storage and utilization. Here we report a novel strategy in which carbon dots (CDs) evenly distributed into polyethylene glycol (PEG) act as local self-heating agents to shorten thermal transfer distances. As a result, CDs enhance the phase change enthalpies of PEG-related PCMs by their interface interaction, resulting in 13.7% higher than the theoretical value. Moreover, the hybrids of CDs and PEG are impregnated into the melamine sponge modified with CDs and polydimethylsiloxane through vacuum induced infiltration technique to prevent the liquid leakage. The obtained composite shows the long heat storage duration and achieves 82.57% of solar-to-thermal storage efficiency as well as 92.27% of the heat release efficiency. In particular, its outstanding hydrophobicity and stability in shape and heating–cooling cycles allows it to practically apply in solar-thermal energy conversion and storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change materials
Solar-thermal energy conversion
Heat storage
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation methods and photothermal storage testing
The combination of CDs with PEG
The confinement of PCMs with sponges
Mechanism of the interaction of CDs with PEG
Photothermal conversion and storage of CDs@PEG/CSponge
Roles of CDs in photothermal conversion and storage process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of CDs-modulated phase change thermal storage capacity.

(a and b) DSC curves and the measured phase change enthalpy of pure PEG6000 and CDs@PEG with the mass ratio of PEG:CDs = 100: 1; (c) Comparisons of phase change enthalpy changes of CDs@PEG with those of PEG-related PCMs reported in literatures. (d) FTIR spectra of PEG6000 and CDs@PEG. (e) In situ FTIR spectra of CDs@PEG during heating. (f) Comparisons of phase change enthalpy changes of CDs@PEG and r-CDs@PEG. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of preparing CDs@PEG/CSponge; (b) Photographs of the pure PEG piece and CDs@PEG/CSponge during heating processes; (c) SEM images of the CDs@PEG/CSponge. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of the home-made setup for testing photothermal conversion and storage performances; (b) The collected temperature–time curves of samples under solar irradiation; (c) Schematic illustration of photothermal conversion and storage mechanism of CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge; (d) Illustration of the effects of interface states on band gaps of CDs.

(a) The temperature–time curves of CDs@PEG/CSponge under outdoor natural sunlight. Testing condition in Taiyuan city, China (Nov. 30th, 2021): The natural sunlight density was 94–106 mW•cm−2 and the ambient temperature was around 6 °C. (b) The photographs of floating on the water of CDs@PEG/CSponge; (c and d) The photographs of drops on the side surface and top surface of CDs@PEG/CSponge; (e) The photothermal conversion and storage cycling performance of the CDs@PEG/CSponge (Upper) and the photos before and after multiple lighting-cooling cycles (Bottom). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the home-made setup for testing photothermal conversion and storage performances.
Thermophysical parameters of the samples from DSC and the calculated efficiencies.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots efficiently enhance photothermal conversion and storage of organic phase change materials through interfacial interaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer phase change materials (PCMs) often suffer from the attenuation of thermal storage capacities and the shrinkage of storage duration after adding conventional high heat conduction matters, resulting in low efficiency of heat storage and utilization. Here we report a novel strategy in which carbon dots (CDs) evenly distributed into polyethylene glycol (PEG) act as local self-heating agents to shorten thermal transfer distances. As a result, CDs enhance the phase change enthalpies of PEG-related PCMs by their interface interaction, resulting in 13.7% higher than the theoretical value. Moreover, the hybrids of CDs and PEG are impregnated into the melamine sponge modified with CDs and polydimethylsiloxane through vacuum induced infiltration technique to prevent the liquid leakage. The obtained composite shows the long heat storage duration and achieves 82.57% of solar-to-thermal storage efficiency as well as 92.27% of the heat release efficiency. In particular, its outstanding hydrophobicity and stability in shape and heating–cooling cycles allows it to practically apply in solar-thermal energy conversion and storage.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Sponge modified with CDs and PDMS

ce:section.ce:para: Although solar radiation can supply abundant heat energy during the daytime, most of it is wasted as a low-grade heat below 100 °C [1]. If such solar-converted heat energy can be stored and utilized efficiently, not only the mismatch between heat supply and demand in terms of time, space, and intensity in building heating and industrial process is promisingly solved, but also the current issue of energy and environmental can be significantly alleviated through reducing the consumption of fossil fuels [1–3]. The thermal energy storage means that uses phase change materials (PCMs) for latent heat storage is considered an ideal technique because of its high storage density in a small temperature region [4]. Compared to inorganic PCMs, organic PCMs are the most popular in the practical applications and have been extensively investigated in the fabrication of high-performance multifunctional hybrid PCMs [4,5]. Even so, so far organic PCMs still face some bottleneck concerns—i.e., short storage duration, weak light absorption capacity for solar-to-thermal conversion, relatively low thermal conductivity for phonon transmission, liquid leakage and heat degradation during one charging/discharging cycle [1,6]. In particular, their low thermal conductivity makes heat transfer inadequate and prevents heat from penetrating into their interior, thus lowering their effectiveness in thermal energy storage applications [4]. Currently many efforts such as the addition of high heat conduction nanomaterials, confinement strategy, have been made to enhance the thermal conductivity of PCMs [7]. Unfortunately, these means often cause not only the attenuation of thermal storage capacities but also the shrinkage of storage duration owing to the improved thermal conductivity of PCMs.

Carbon dots (CDs) consist of carbon cores surrounded by the terminal functional groups such as carbonyl groups, carboxylic acids, and alcohols [8–10]. The surface terminal groups of CDs indeed regulate their interaction with the surrounding environment in aspects of reactivity, recognition and binding, solvation as well as the material processability, endowing their unique molecular-like behaviors [8,11–13]. Thereby CDs stand as the missing link between the molecular and the nanoscale world and can easily merge into the organic materials for reforming their microstructure and physical properties including of mechanical, optical, and thermal properties comparing to other low-dimensional carbon materials [14]. Moreover, CDs possess broadband absorption in the ultraviolet–visible region; in particular, such property can be effectively modulated by interface states formed with themselves and other materials [15–18]. Therefore, CDs enable the photothermal effect by the process that excited electrons return to the low-level states, which is accompanied by local heating of the lattice and phonon transmission energy release [19].

In this work, we present a novel strategy in which CDs evenly distributed into organic polyethylene glycol (PEG) act as local self-heating agents to reduce thermal transfer distance as illustrated in Fig. 1 . As a result, PEG molecules can be allowed to fast charge solar-converted heat without suffering the burden of storage duration degradation and their thermal storage capacity exceeds their theoretical value due to the interaction of CDs and PEG molecules, significantly enhancing solar-to-heat energy storage. After the hybrids of CDs and PEG are impregnated into the commercial melamine sponge modified with CDs and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) through vacuum induced infiltration process, the composite PCMs not only prevent the leakage problem of PEG, but also exhibit the enhanced solar-to-thermal conversion efficiency and excellent stability in shape and heating–cooling cycles. As thus, this novel photothermal storage composite PCMs are promising to become an ideal candidate for the practical solar thermal storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation methods and photothermal storage testing

ce:section.ce:para: Commercial melamine sponge were purchased from Ledian furniture company of China without further treatment. The used chemical reagents, such as PDMS, ethyl acetate (EA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, ≥99.8%), formic acid (HCOOH, 88 v/v%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 v/v%), PEG with average molecular weights of 1000, 6000 and 10000 labeled as PEG1000, PEG6000 and PEG10000 respectively, were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. All these reagents were directly used with no further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The combination of CDs with PEG
ce:section.ce:para: The used CDs were prepared by selective oxidation etching method using coal tar pitch as precursors and the mixture of hydrogen peroxide and formic acid as the oxidizing agent with a final optimization reported in our previous work [18]. Additionally, the reduction of CDs with NaBH4 (r-CDs) was based on the previously reported method [20], which was also described in Supplementary Material. PEG (2 g) and a certain amount of as-prepared CDs under different mass ratios were dispersed in 4 mL of dichloromethane by intensely stirring for 1.5 h and further ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h, then the dispersion was placed in a vacuum drying oven under vacuum (−0.8 Mpa) and 70 °C to completely remove dichloromethane. After cooling the room temperature, the hybrids of carbon dots and PEG (CDs@PEG) containing different contents of CDs were obtained. By the same processes, the r-CDs@PEG was prepared by replacing CDs with r-CDs as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The confinement of PCMs with sponges
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previously reported method [18], commercial melamine sponge was functionalized by CDs and PDMS to obtain the surface-modified sponge (named as CSponge) for further use. Typically, PEG (1 g) and CDs (0.01 g) with a mass ratio of 100:1 were dispersed in 4 mL of dichloromethane using intensively stirring for 1.5 h and further ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the pristine sponge or CSponge was soaked in the resultant solution under vacuum (−0.8 Mpa) and 70 °C to conduct adsorption through negative pressure and capillary force. After 2 h, the pristine sponge or CSponge was taken out and cooling to room temperature to obtain CDs@PEG/Sponge or CDs@PEG/CSponge. Similarly, the PEG/Sponge was prepared using the same processes mentioned above.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , a solar simulator (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, China) with an intensity of 110 mW m−2 was employed in the experiment, and the surface area of the PCM sample placed on a home-made thermal insulation foam was 4.2 cm2. A thermocouple (KS-SAT03-10) connecting to the bottom of the sample and temperature recorder was used to record the temperature change in this experiment.
Other experimental details and sample characterizations were provided in the Supplementary Material.

We first performed X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization of the PEG6000 before and after adding CDs, and found that the crystallization of PEG6000 was not affected by the addition of CDs (Fig. S1a). In particular, the addition of CDs did not improve thermal conductivity of PEG6000 (Fig. S1b). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were performed to evaluate the melting and freezing enthalpies of the PEG6000 before and after adding CDs according to the endothermic and exothermic processes. Fig. 2 a shows the heating and freezing curves of PEG6000 with and without CDs. In the absence of the CDs, the melting enthalpy (ΔHm) and crystallization enthalpy (ΔHc) of the pure PEG6000 were 190.24 J g−1 and 172.28 J g−1, respectively. The contents of CDs added in PEG6000 can remarkably influence the phase change enthalpies of the CDs@PEG (Fig. S2). When the mass ratio of PEG6000 and CDs is 100:1, CDs enable the ΔHm and ΔHc of PEG6000 to reach 216.28 and 189.26 J g−1, which is 13.7% and 9.9% higher than those of the pure PEG6000, respectively (Fig. 2b). Moreover, Fig. 2c summarizes the changes of phase transition enthalpy of PEG with different composite systems reported in literatures [11,21–28]. In comparison, the CDs@PEG shows the highest value. The increase of phase change enthalpy for the CDs@PEG could be attributed to the interaction of C-H bonds in PEG with the surface groups of CDs, affecting the melting and crystallization processes of PEG similar with the reports in literature [26]. Accordingly, we determined the interaction of CDs with PEG by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the interaction of CDs with PEG
ce:section.ce:para: From FTIR spectra (Fig. S3), one can observe the variations of chemical structures before and after adding CDs into PEG. As for PEG, the characteristic peaks at 2888, 1342 and 1057 cm−1 assigning to the bonds of C-H, C-CH2 and CH2-O-H respectively, display a slight shift towards larger wavenumbers after adding CDs (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, a new peak at 1132 cm−1 in accordance with the chemical bonds of C-O-C/C-O-H is observed as well, which could derive from the consequence of the interaction of CDs with PEGs. Furthermore, in situ FTIR during heating processes was used to further identify the effects of CDs on the phase transition process (Fig. 2e). As the temperature rises, the characteristic peak at 1132 and 1064 cm−1 in CDs@PEG gradually disappears due to the melting of PEG. This reveals that the interaction of PEG molecules with CDs relies on the chemical bonds of C-O-C/C-O-H. To further confirm it, the CDs were selectively reduced with sodium borohydride, which enables the carbonyl to transform into hydroxyl groups without reducing other species (C=C and COOH), based on the method reported in literature [20] and the obtained sample named as r-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2f, it can be found that the r-CDs do not make the phase change enthalpy of PEG increase, but decrease. All above results demonstrate that CDs can interact with C-H bonds in PEG molecules through delocalized π electrons by virtue of carbonyl groups on their surface, leading to new bonds (i.e. C=O‧‧‧C/C=O‧‧‧H) analogous to C-O-C/C-O-H (as illustrated in Fig. 1). As thus, the solid-liquid phase change process of PEG is influenced by CDs and gives rise to the enhanced latent heat storage performance.

Moreover, we explored the effects of CDs on phase change enthalpies of different average molecule weights of PEG (Fig. S4). It can be found that the optimal amount of CDs in CDs@PEG is related to molecule weights of PEG. According to Figs. S2 and S4, we plotted the relationship between the average molecule weight of PEG and the corresponding optimal content of CDs (Fig. S5), revealing that the more molecule weight of PEG usually allows more CDs to achieve ideal latent heat storage capacity. This could be attributed to the differences of molecule-weight-induced moveability, thereby requiring different numbers of CDs to prevent intermolecular motions.

In general, the pristine melamine sponge has a very limited absorption for sunlight as well as a hydrophilic property. To improve photon capturing ability and avoid the leakage of PEG dissolution, the melamine sponge was functionalized with CDs and PDMS to acquire CSponge, which was endowed with new unique properties of excellent light-harvesting capacity (Fig. S6) and superior hydrophobicity. Fig. 3 a depicts the confining CDs@PEG process with CSponge to prepare composite PCMs of the CDs@PEG/CSponge. Under vacuum induced infiltration action, the mixed suspension of CDs@PEG was impregnated into CSponge, then obtaining composite PCMs with a maximum 97.14 wt% PEG loading after completely removing solvent.

When the PCMs were placed in an oven at 70 °C for 10 min, the melting phenomenon of pure PEG piece was clearly observed, whereas no liquid leakage took place for CDs@PEG/CSponge under the same condition (Fig. 3b). This indicates that CDs@PEG/CSponge possesses good shape stability and can efficiently prevent the problematic contact of liquid phase of PEG with surrounding environment. Fig. 3c shows the representative scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the CDs@PEG/CSponge at different magnifications. It is clear that CDs@PEG has been well filled in CSponge pores, but some gaps are present between the crystalline PEG, which can provide excess space for the solid-liquid phase transition of PEG. As thus, the leakage is avoided with the help of capillary force.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal conversion and storage of CDs@PEG/CSponge
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal conversion and storage performance of the composite PCMs was tested through home-made setup with a simulated sunlight source, which can directly irradiate on the sample in the foam box (Scheme 1). During the sunlight radiation, the composites PCMs generally absorb light-converted heat and the temperature rises definitely. As the temperature reaches the melting point of PCMs, an inflection point comes out in the temperature-time curves, suggesting that the phase change process and thermal energy storage occur with a little fluctuation of the temperature (Fig. 4 a). When the irradiation is turn off, the temperature of the samples will drop down until their temperature runs up to the crystallizing point. After that, the temperature–time curve shows an inflection as the PCMs solidify. When the phase change process is finished, the curve resumes their fast downward trend. For comparison, the temperature–time curves of the PEG/Sponge, CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge samples were collected as shown in Fig. 4a and b. It can be found that CDs@PEG/CSponge gives rise to the highest temperature under the same irradiation time (Fig. 4a). Because of the poor light absorption for pure PEG, its highest temperature can merely reach to 55 °C, which remains lower than its phase change temperature, under solar irradiation. However, the maximum temperature of both CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge samples can exceed the phase change temperature of PEG under the same conditions, presenting a typical phase change platform. The difference is that the phase change of CDs@PEG/CSponge occurs faster than that of CDs@PEG/Sponge thanks to its better sunlight harvesting ability (Fig. 4b). More notably, the duration of crystallizing process is much longer than that of melting process for CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge samples, implying their superior thermal storage duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Roles of CDs in photothermal conversion and storage process
ce:section.ce:para: The roles of CDs played in photothermal conversion and storage process are depicted in Fig. 4c and d. The CDs dispersing into PEG can only absorb the incident photons that their energy matches or exceeds the band gap of CDs. Initially, the energy of the incident photons is stored in the CDs by photoexcitation. Then, the input energy dissipates in the form of heat or emitted light via the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The generated heat is further transferred to the surrounding PEG molecules, realizing the thermal energy storage through phase change process (The left of Fig. 4c). If the channel walls of sponge are functionalized by using CDs, the resultant CSponge is able to promote sunlight harvesting and conversion by means of the interface states with smaller band gaps created by the contact of CDs as illustrated in the bottom of Fig. 4d. Besides, the emitted light from CDs surrounded by PEG may be reabsorbed by the CDs on the CSponge and further converts into thermal energy to heating PEG in the pores of sponge (The right of Fig. 4c). Therefore, CDs@PEG/CSponge shows the faster photothermal conversion and storage capacity than CDs@PEG/Sponge.

Additionally, we qualitatively determined the photothermal storage efficiencies (η) of CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge samples using the following Equation (1) reported in literature [29]:
(1)
η =
m
Δ H
m
P S
(
t t
−
t f
)
× 100 %
where P, S, m, and △H
m are the power density of the simulated solar irradiation, the illumination area, the mass and the melting enthalpy of the composite PCMs, respectively. The starting and terminating time (t
t and t
f ) of the solar-driven phase change are obtained by the tangential method (Fig. S7). The details of the required parameters for calculations are shown in Table S1. Consequently, the photothermal storage efficiencies of CDs@PEG/Sponge and CDs@PEG/CSponge are 62.75% and 82.57%, respectively (Table 1 ). It is clear that about 20% increase of photothermal storage efficiency is achieved after modifying sponge with CDs. Of critical importance is the heat release efficiency (HRR) of composite PCMs, which is defined as the proportion of the useable part and the total stored thermal energy and can be calculated by the following equation [30]:
(2)
H R R =
(
1 −
Δ H
m
−
Δ H
c
Δ H
m
)
× 100 %

From Table 1, the HRR values of pure PEG is 90.56% and 88.45% before and after confining in sponges, respectively. In contrast, CDs@PEG shows the higher HRR, more than 92%, due to the contribution of CDs for crystallization enthalpy of PEG. To further clarify it, we also introduce the relative enthalpy efficiency (γ) concept, which can be calculated by the following function [31]:
(3)
γ =
Δ H
m
(
C o m p o s i t e
)
+
Δ H
c
(
C o m p o s i t e
)
(
Δ H
m
(
P E G
)
+
Δ H
c
(
P E G
)
)
w
× 100 %
where
Δ H
m
(
C o m p o s i t e
)
and
Δ H
c
(
C o m p o s i t e
)
are the melting and crystallization enthalpy of the composite PCMs, respectively;
Δ H
m
(
P E G
)
and
Δ H
c
(
P E G
)
are the melting and crystallization enthalpy of the PEG, respectively; w is the mass fraction of the PEG in the composites. The calculated results are provided in Table 1 as well, it can be found that the supporting sponge reduces the phase change enthalpy of the PEG, but introducing CDs into PEG can increase γ and even exceeds the theoretical value of PEG. All of above results verify that CDs efficiently improve the photothermal conversion and storage performance of organic PCMs.

In consideration of practical application, CDs@PEG/CSponge was placed on the square of the Engineering Training Center at the North University of China. The experiment was carried out under natural sunlight with solar heat flux of 94–106 mW•cm−2 and air temperature of around 6 °C. Fig. 5 a provides the measured temperature-time curves, indicating the outstanding photo-thermal conversion and storage performance. More importantly, the CSponge is endowed with excellent hydrophobicity due to the CDs and PDMS modification, which increases surface roughness of the sponge. As depicted in Fig. 5b–d, CDs@PEG/CSponge presents superior hydrophobic functions as well, enabling it to float on the water and avoiding the dissolution of PEG while contacting water. In order to examine thermal reliability and structure stability of the CDs@PEG/CSponge, we further carried out the cycling experiment of photo-thermal storage performance. From Fig. 5e, there are no obvious differences in temperature-time curves and morphology features under multiple lighting-cooling cycles. As a result, the CDs@PEG/CSponge possesses outstanding stability and recyclability and meets the requirements of solar-thermal energy conversion and storage materials in practical use.

To make full utilization of solar energy, we report a CDs-modulated PEG composite that can efficiently store thermal energy using sunlight absorption. It has been demonstrated that CDs can enhance the phase change enthalpies of PEG-related PCMs by their interface interaction, resulting in 13.7% higher than the theoretical value under optimized mass ratio of CDs and PEG. After the hybrids of CDs@PEG are impregnated into the CSponge through vacuum induced infiltration technique, the composite CDs@PEG/CSponge not only prevents the leakage of liquid PEG, but also achieves 82.57% of solar-to-thermal storage efficiency and 92.27% of the heat release efficiency. In particular, the CDs@PEG/CSponge also exhibits the outstanding stability in shape and heating–cooling cycles, meeting the needs of practical applications for solar-thermal energy conversion and storage.

Jinlong Li: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qing Chang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Chaorui Xue: Investigation, Visualization. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136) and Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.11.046.
